coredata.dc:title: Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 coupled with biomass-derived carbon dots for promoted bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction: Mechanism elucidation of intensified energy metabolism

coredata.dc:description: Bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) has been touted as one of the promising strategies to convert CO2 to high-value chemicals via the efficient microbial electrosynthesis (MES). However, the sluggish electron transfer efficiency between microbes and cathodes hampers the CO2 reduction efficiency. Herein, biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) with high electrical conductivity were coupled with Shewanella oneidensis (S. oneidensis) MR-1 to construct a nanomaterial-bacterial biohybrid system to promote CO2-to-formate formation. Specifically, the corncob-derived CDs (cc-CDs) were highly-biocompatible with S. oneidensis MR-1, with such a constructed biohybrid system showing a marked formate titer of 0.50 mmol/L. Upon doping with Fe element, the resultant Fe-cc-CDs exhibited a considerably boosted formate titer of 1.22 mmol/L, which was 7.7 times higher than the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system. Such significant CO2-to-formate conversion enhancement was contributed by the internalized CDs in S. oneidensis MR-1 stimulating extracellular electron transfer efficiency, therefore accelerating energy metabolism for formate bioelectrosynthesis. The intensified energy metabolism in S. oneidensis MR-1 was corroborated by the increased concentrations of ATP, NADH and overexpression of energy-related genes. This work demonstrates an intensified energy metabolism exemplification for CO2 reduction, which can be expanded to a host of different nanomaterials-internalized MES systems for bioelectrochemical implications.

(b) Schematic illustrations of the constructed CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system for boosted bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction, owing to CDs-induced intensified metabolism with abundant energy provision for formate dehydrogenase to conduct CO2-to-formate conversion.

Physicochemical characterizations of cc-CDs, N,S-cc-CDs and Fe-cc-CDs. Particle size distribution of cc-CDs with the photoluminescence image collected at 365 nm imbedded; (a), N,S-cc-CDs (b) and Fe-cc-CDs (c). Zeta potential (d), UV–vis absorption spectra (e) and FTIR spectra (f) of cc-CDs, N,S-cc-CDs and Fe-cc-CDs.

Morphological characterization of the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system and the biohybrid systems. TEM and HR-TEM images of the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system (a), cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system (b), N,S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system (c) and Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system (d). CLSM images of S. oneidensis MR-1 and biohybrid systems (e). The green signals indicate those from live cells; the red signals indicate those from dead cells.

(a) Schematic description of the biohybrid system for bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction. (b) Growth curves from the biohybrid systems in a fresh anaerobic medium. Formate concentration in the catholyte during MES (c, d, e); Biomass obtained from carbon felt after 36 h operation (f, g, h).
Cyclic voltammetry (a) and EIS (b) curves of pure S. oneidensis MR-1, cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1, N,S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 and Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 systems.

Metabolic product analyses after the bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction. The concentration of NADtal, NADH and NAD+ (a), the ratio of NADH/NAD+ (b), and intracellular ATP level (c) in pure S. oneidensis MR-1, cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1, N,S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 and Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 systems.

The expression levels of genes in N,S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 and Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems. Real-time qRT-PCR results of the Mtr module (a) and energy module (b) in the N, S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system. Real-time qRT-PCR results of the Mtr module (c) and energy module (d) in Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system.
Schematic demonstration of the intensified energy metabolism mechanism in the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems for enhanced bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient abiotic-biotic system constructed by CDs-integrated S. oneidensis MR-1 was utilized to enhance the bioelectrochemical CO2-to-formate conversion. The biomass-derived CDs exhibited promising biocompatibility with S. oneidensis MR-1, which were even being internalized inside the S. oneidensis MR-1, thus leading to strengthened extracellular electron transfer efficiency and intensified energy metabolism for a promoted CO2-to-formate conversion.

Bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) has been touted as one of the promising strategies to convert CO2 to high-value chemicals via the efficient microbial electrosynthesis (MES). However, the sluggish electron transfer efficiency between microbes and cathodes hampers the CO2 reduction efficiency. Herein, biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) with high electrical conductivity were coupled with Shewanella oneidensis (S. oneidensis) MR-1 to construct a nanomaterial-bacterial biohybrid system to promote CO2-to-formate formation. Specifically, the corncob-derived CDs (cc-CDs) were highly-biocompatible with S. oneidensis MR-1, with such a constructed biohybrid system showing a marked formate titer of 0.50 mmol/L. Upon doping with Fe element, the resultant Fe-cc-CDs exhibited a considerably boosted formate titer of 1.22 mmol/L, which was 7.7 times higher than the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system. Such significant CO2-to-formate conversion enhancement was contributed by the internalized CDs in S. oneidensis MR-1 stimulating extracellular electron transfer efficiency, therefore accelerating energy metabolism for formate bioelectrosynthesis. The intensified energy metabolism in S. oneidensis MR-1 was corroborated by the increased concentrations of ATP, NADH and overexpression of energy-related genes. This work demonstrates an intensified energy metabolism exemplification for CO2 reduction, which can be expanded to a host of different nanomaterials-internalized MES systems for bioelectrochemical implications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive CO2 emission in the atmosphere leads to severely environmental issues, particularly the world-widely concerned climate change(Wan et al., 2021). Consequently, adopting a methodology to efficiently reduce CO2 into value-added chemicals or fuels is of high urgency to address such concerns, concurrently realizing a green/sustainable societal development and a circular carbon economy (Liu et al., 2022; Tan and Nielsen, 2022). As such, thermochemical, electrochemical, photochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) and their integrated tandem methods have been extensively explored with promising progresses. Recently, bioelectrochemical CO2RR has been touted as an effective way for CO2 transformation, hence, drawing ever-growing researching attention on seeking an ideal nanomaterial-bacterial biohybrid system to maximize the CO2 utilization efficiency. Specifically, the bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction methodology utilizes the microbial electrosynthesis (MES) process to convert CO2 to value-added biochemicals, showing the advantages of high efficiency, low costs and operation simplicity (Butler and Lovley, 2016; Gavilanes et al., 2019; Gupta et al., 2021; Yadav et al., 2022). On this basis, several microorganisms have been applied to the MES, including Shewanella, Geobacter, Clostridium and Sporomusa (Chen et al., 2020; Li et al., 2023a), in which the Shewanella represents one of the most potential microorganisms for MES, due to its excellent extracellular electron transfer (EET) efficiency.

Despite Shewanella has drawn intensive research interests, the EET efficiency between the electroactive microorganisms and electrodes is still ultralow and remains unsatisfactory, therefore hindering its practical applications (Lekshmi et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2020). To improve the EET efficiency, researchers in general utilize two main strategies: through either the optimization of electrode materials or the modification of microorganisms (Modestra and Mohan, 2019; Rengasamy et al., 2018; Song et al., 2017; Suo et al., 2020). Accrediting to the operation simplicity and well-known mechanism, significant progress has been made in optimizing electrode materials. Nevertheless, this tactic is still unable to tackle the low EET efficiency issue between microorganisms and electrodes (Prevoteau et al., 2020). Fortunately, a biotic-abiotic electrosynthesis has been regarded as a promising and effective biohybrid system for upgrading the MES process, as it integrates the merits of inorganic electrode materials with the self-repairable whole-cell biocatalysts (namely the microorganisms) (Han et al., 2022). Notwithstanding, such a biohybrid system has not been extensively explored for bioelectrochemical CO2 conversion. The frequently reported biotic-abiotic biohybrid systems include CdS-S. oneidensis MR-1, CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 and Cus-S. oneidensis MR-1, which integrate the remarkable electron absorption ability of inorganic nanomaterials with the highly biocatalytic activity of microbes. Notably, these biotic-abiotic biohybrid systems outperform either the individual chemical or biological route in terms of electron transfer, therefore, significantly suppressing the interfacial electron transfer barrier between microorganisms and electrodes, in turn leading to an improved EET efficiency and MSE performance (Liu et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023).

As a matter of fact, it is renowned that the advantages of inorganic nanomaterials in a biotic-abiotic electrosynthesis system are attributed to their highly specific electroconductibility, which serve as electron shuttles between electrodes and microorganisms (Li et al., 2023a). Noteworthily, a previous case revealed that graphitic nitrogen doped carbon dots (g-N-CDs) and MtrC from S. oneidensis MR-1 enabled to construct a covalent biohybrid system leading to improved electron transfer efficiency. Essentially, this case also pointed out that g-N-CDs enabled to transfer electrons to MtrC (that is a c-type cytochrome in Mtr pathway), indicating the high possibility of using g-N-CDs to trigger the transmembrane electron transfer in S. oneidensis MR-1 (Zhang et al., 2023a). Importantly, as the Mtr pathway will generate massive energies for metabolism, therefore, it is anticipated that the utilization of such a biotic-abiotic biohybrid system will improve the EET efficiency, in turn resulting in the generation of more energy for metabolism (Hou et al., 2020). This hypothesis was verified by a CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system, in which the EET efficiency was indeed considerably improved and the expression of related genes was detected (Li et al., 2023b; Yang et al., 2020). Clearly, these exemplified cases elucidated that CDs can be bio-incorporated with the S. oneidensis MR-1 to accelerate the bacterial metabolism, especially in an energy-consuming metabolism scenario or a cytochrome c system. Another study pointed out that the CdS-S. oneidensis MR-1 photosynthetic system was able to reverse the Mtr pathway, leading to significant ATP upregulation and reducing equivalent of NADH/NAD+ upregulation (Han et al., 2022). Since the reversed Mtr pathway results in the generation of a proton motive force for ATP and NADH synthesis (Rowe et al., 2018), it can be hypothesized here that the energy module-relevant genes may be up-regulated when a biotic-abiotic biohybrid system is used for the energy-consuming bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction.

Inspired by previous studies, in an upgraded biotic-abiotic biohybrid system, we anticipate that a suitable nanomaterial with high electroconductivity and biocompatibility coupled with S. oneidensis MR-1 will largely enhance the EET efficiency, in turn significantly activating the energy metabolism process to boost MES for CO2 conversion. It is noteworthy that carbon dots (CDs) have not been explored in an abiotic-Shewanella biohybrid system for bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction yet, and whether energy metabolism will be intensified in such a biohybrid system and how to be intensified also have not been demonstrated. To fill in the gaps and validate the hypotheses, as such, in this work, a serial of CDs derived from straw, corncob and peanut shell (denoted as ss-CDs, cc-CDs and ps-CDs correspondingly) were synthesized to configure the CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems to explore their bioelectrochemical CO2RR performance. In addition, to maximize the CO2-to-formate conversion efficiency, the biomass-derived CDs were further optimized by doping different elements, rendering the doped CDs showing a significantly boosted MES efficiency and increased formate concentration. Such boosted CO2-to-formate conversion in the CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system was then systematically unveiled, due to CDs-induced intensified energy metabolism. This work demonstrates the first example of incorporation highly-conductive CDs with S. oneidensis MR-1 to improve the EET efficiency for a strengthened CO2-to-formate conversion, which can be extended to a broad spectrum of bioelectrochemistry-associated implications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, purification and modification of the biomass-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Three types of biomass powders were utilized to synthesized CDs in this study, including straw, peanut shell and corncob. Specifically, 2 g of biomass powders were previously sieved by 100 sieve mesh and then the sieved particles were placed in a PTFE hydrothermal reactor (100 mL) containing 50 mL of ultrapure water, followed by a hydrothermal treatment at 220℃ for 12 h. When the hydrothermal reaction was completed, the solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane for removing the impurities. Then, a dialysis bag with a cut-off molecular weight of 500 was used in the filtrate to purify the biomass-derived CDs solutions, leading to the final formation of purified CDs (as shown in Fig. 1 a). For simplicity, the straw-derived carbon dots, peanut shell-derived carbon dots and corncob-derived carbon dots were denoted as ss-CDs, ps-CDs and cc-CDs, respectively. As for doping, 2 g of corncob powders were mixed with 50 mL of solution containing 1 g of ammonium persulfate or 1 g of ferric ammonium sulfate dodecahydrate, followed by the aforementioned hydrothermal treatments and purification steps to form the resultant N,S-cc-CDs and Fe-cc-CDs, respectively. All the as-synthesized CDs powders were obtained by freeze-drying and redispersed in deionized water for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and identification of CDs-S. Oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems
ce:section.ce:para: S. oneidensis MR-1 was separately inoculated in aerobic and anaerobic cultures for further utilization. Aerobic cultures were inoculated in Luria–Bertani (LB) broth at 30 °C by shaking at 200 r/min. The anaerobic culture medium was improved on the basis of a previous report to have 15.0 mmol/L sodium lactate as the electron donor and 40.0 mmol/L fumarate as the electron acceptor (pH 7.0) (Li et al., 2019). To eliminate oxygen, before sterilization, 50 mL of Shewanella anaerobic culture medium was first transferred into a 105 mL serum bottle by purging N2 for 1 h. The sterilization procedure was then performed with the culture medium sealed in a serum bottle with rubber stoppers and aluminium seals. After sterilization, S. oneidensis MR-1 was incubated in 50 mL of culture medium in an incubator at 30 ℃.

To construct the S. oneidensis MR-1-CDs biohybrid systems, two milliliters of logarithmic phase S. oneidensis MR-1 were aerobically cultivated overnight in 50 mL LB broth at 30 °C on a shaker (200 rpm). After washing twice using Shewanella anaerobic culture medium, the cells were transferred into 50 mL of Shewanella anaerobic culture medium for another 8 h anaerobic culture at 30 °C on a shaker (200 rpm). At this stage, the specific type of CDs filtrated by 0.22 μm filter membrane was added to the medium to preliminarily form the S. oneidensis MR-1-CDs biohybrid systems (Yang et al., 2020). Then the solution was stationarily cultivated for another 4 h to finally form the S. oneidensis MR-1-CDs biohybrid system. The morphology of microorganisms and CDs in the CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems was identified by the transmission electron microscope (TEM) according to a previously reported method (Liu et al., 2020). The bacterial solution after the reaction was cleaned and fixed overnight with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde at 4 °C, then the new-obtained samples were attained by the agar casting method, namely the dehydrated and epoxy-embedded samples. After that, samples were sliced into 50–100 nm with an ultramicrotome, followed by placing on a 200-mesh copper mesh for image taking at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

The viability of cells was assayed by using LIVE/DEAD™ BacLight™ bacterial activity assay kit, according to differential permeability of fluorescent dye (Katuri et al., 2011). The incubated cells were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min and washed three times by centrifuging with 0.9 % NaCl to remove the media, followed by suspending in 100 μL 0.9 % NaCl, then 100 μL of the dye mixture (SYTO 9/propidium iodide, 1:1) was added into cells and stained for 20 min in dark. After incubation, the samples were washed by centrifugation with buffer and through a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM). SYTO 9 signals were detected using an excitation wavelength of 488 nm and an emission wavelength of 503 nm. The fluorescence agent (propidium iodide) was excited at 543 nm and the emission was collected through a long-band pass filter at 605 nm. The viability of CDs-coated bacterial cells was examined following the same procedure disclosed above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Set up and operation of MES
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatible carbon felt (2 × 2 × 0.2 cm) was used as to load the CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biofilm. Specifically, the carbon felt was immersed in 100 mL single-chamber MFC Shewanella anaerobic culture medium of previously cultivated CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems for 16 h to form biofilm, and an applied voltage of −1.2 V was used to promote the rapid growth of biofilms. After the biofilm fabricated, the carbon felt was transferred into the dual-chamber MFC for the following experiments.

A dual-chamber bioelectrochemical system (dual-chamber MFC, 50 mL) separated by Nafion 117 (DuPont, USA) was used in this study with a 2 KΩ resistor as the external circuit load (Song et al., 2022). In this MES system, the cathodic electrode was the fabricated biofilm and the anodic electrode was the titanium (1 cm × 2 cm). The anode chamber contained 50 mL solution of 100 mM KCl and 1 mM H2SO4. Voltages were recorded every 10 min by a Keithely data collector. For CO2RR, at initial, 10 L/min pure CO2 (Xinhang, China, Assay ≥ 99.5 %) was irrigated in the cathodic chamber for 6 h. Meanwhile, a constant pre-designed potential was obtained by using a potentiostat.

For the production determination, formate concentration was quantified by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis using C18 (Agilent, USA) column with absorption 210 nm. BCA technology was used to determine the biomass on the cathode (Luo et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2022). All the experiments were performed in triplicate.

All electrochemical tests were performed immediately after the MES reaction. Electrochemical tests were performed by a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation in a typical three-electrode system with an Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode (Liu et al., 2020). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) at a scan rate of 10 mV/s and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz was used to determine the electrochemically active surface area (Habibi et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021b).

In this study, the concentrations of NADH, NAD+ and ATP at the end of reaction were measured using NAD+/NADH and ATP assay kit (Shanghai Biyuntian Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China) (Sun et al., 2022). The relative abundances of S. oneidensis MR-1 were evaluated using SYBR-green-based real-time qRT-PCR. The primers used in this study were showed in Table S1. For real-time qRT-PCR analysis, total RNA was extracted, purified and then reverse-transcribed to cDNA. To uniformly quantify the copy number, cDNA used as the template was previously diluted to 100 ng/μL after measuring the concentration of cDNA by the DNA Analyzer (Quawell, USA). 20 μL was used to perform real-time qRT-PCR by Step-One-Plus real-time qPCR System (Applied Biosystems, USA). The copy numbers of gene fragments were used to evaluate the abundances of functional genes while the recA gene was utilized as a reference gene owing to its relatively stable expression. Each sample was quantified in triplicates.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were tested on silicon by a SmartLab-SE Phaser Diffractometer (Fuso, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis was performed on a HITACHI S-4800 (Hitach, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 12 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was measured on an electron microscope (Tecnai F30, FEI), at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The preparation of samples was showed in “Synthesis and identification of CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems”. Zeta potential was performed on Malvern Nanoparticle Potential Analyzer (ZEN 3600) with PBS solution as solvent. The Zeta potential was calculated by Smoluchowski equation.

The detailed procedures regarding the synthesis of CDs from biomass are profiled in Fig. 1a, where only very simple steps are used. The constructed CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system for bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction is illustrated in Fig. 1b, where the mutual biocompatibility between CDs and S. oneidensis MR-1 and the subsequent formation of biofilm on carbon felt as cathode are prerequisites for an enhanced bioelectrochemical CO2-to-formate conversion. Accrediting to CDs-induced intensified energy metabolism, the formate dehydrogenase will receive abundant energy provisions from the Mtr pathway module and energy module to improve CO2 conversion efficiency (Fig. 1b). The detailed material characterizations, bioelectrochemical CO2RR assessments and metabolic mechanism elucidations in various CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems are provided in the following sections.

The morphology, size and composition of the resultant CDs (ss-CDs, ps-CDs and cc-CDs) were systematically characterized using various characterization methods, including microscopic techniques, FTIR spectroscopy and zeta potential. In detail, the diameters of the three biomass-derived CDs ranged from 2.33 nm to 5.56 nm with marked monodispersity (as observed by TEM images, Fig. S1). FTIR spectra substantiated that all the as-synthesized CDs possessed two pronounced stretching vibration modes (Fig. S2), belonging to the CO and N-H groups, indicative of high conductivity (Tian et al., 2021). The ss-CDs, cc-CDs and ps-CDs exhibited similar surface zeta potential, which were respective −10.92 mV, −11.08 mV and −12.11 mV (Fig. S2). These zeta potential values also suggested that the resultant CDs will show similar conductivity.

As analyzed above, all the biomass-derived CDs are suitable nanomaterials to be internalized into cells to compose the CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system for the following bioelectrochemical CO2 conversion, which are confirmed by the HR-TEM images (Fig. S3). As profiled in TEM images (Fig. S3), where some larger black dots are observed on the bacterial surface, with a larger diameter formed ranging from 8 to 20 nm. These observations point out that aggregation occurs only after CDs being internalized, resulting in morphological alternation, in line with previously reported studies (Hudson et al., 2005; Zhang et al., 2023c). These aggregates may contribute from the combination between CDs and bacterial internal components, such as cytochromes, peptides, and enzymes related to electron transfer, respiration, and substrate oxidation.

To comprehensively study the CO2 conversion performance in the biohybrid systems, a two-electrode system was used in this work. Therefore, the electric potential difference was set at −0.7 V referring to the traditional three-electrode system to explore the CO2-to-fromate conversion. Correspondingly, the pre-determined voltage should lie in the electric potential difference of −0.7 V, and a wide range of applied voltages of 0, 0.6, 1.2, 1.8 and 2.4 V were therefore selected (Suo et al., 2019). During the bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction process, the largest accumulated formate was 0.15 mmol/L at an applied potential of 1.2 V (Fig. S4a). As the biomass on biofilm is hard to define, therefore the protein concentration is used to determine biomass in cathode by the bicinchoninic acid (BCA) method. Results indicated that the largest protein concentration was also formed at 1.2 V (Fig. S4b), consequently, 1.2 V was selected as the most favorable potential for the S. oneidensis MR-1 to perform CO2 reduction. In order to find the most suitable S. oneidensis MR-1-CDs biohybrid system for CO2 reduction, 60 mg/L of ss-CDs, cc-CDs and ps-CDs were respectively used. Results showed that the cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system yielded the highest concentration of formate (Fig. S4c). As a result, cc-CDs derived from corncob was the best one to be internalized with S. oneidensis MR-1 to conduct CO2 reduction. With such merits, the addition of cc-CDs may induce the CO2-to-formate conversion, therefore a further experiment regarding the incorporation of cc-CDs onto carbon felt to check its CO2 electrolysis performance was designed. Surprisingly, there was no formate produced in the only cc-CDs-contained (Fig. S5), as a consequence, it can be concluded here that the formate was exclusively produced from the biohybrid system or the cc-CDs enabled to trigger CO2 electrolysis when it is being biohybridized with S. oneidensis MR-1.

Despite the cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system already significantly improved the CO2-to-formate production, a further CO2 reduction efficiency improvement deserves to be exploited. In this sense, element doping is a simple and effective method to optimize the electrocatalytic performances of CDs (Zhang et al., 2023b), as the carbon skeletons of CDs can be readily doped with heteroatoms leading to altered surface groups, more exposed active sites, reduced overelectromotive force and modified electrical conductivity (Li et al., 2021a). Hence, ammonium persulfate was used to modulate the cc-CDs to obtain N and S co-doped cc-CDs (denoted as N,S-cc-CDs), while the ferric ammonium sulfate dodecahydrate was adopted to produce the Fe doped cc-CDs (denoted as Fe-cc-CDs). The physicochemical properties of the doped cc-CDs were also holistically investigated. In detail, N,S-cc-CDs and Fe-cc-CDs showed an average particle size of 2.75 nm (Fig. 2 b) and 3.21 nm (Fig. 2c), respectively, which were larger than the un-doped cc-CDs (2.36 nm, Fig. 2a). The blue-emissive photoluminescence image in Fig. 2a offers visual evidence to support the as-synthesized cc-CDs is of high-quality. As to zeta potential, N,S-cc-CDs (−11.3 mV, Fig. 2d) exhibited an similar value with cc-CDs (−11.1 mV, Fig. 2d). While the value for Fe-cc-CDs was significantly increased to −20.7 mV (Fig. 2d), indicating that the doping of Fe element strongly tailored the electronegativity of cc-CDs. In addition, the successful doping of N and S or Fe into cc-CDs was also verified by the auxiliary XRD, UV–vis and FTIR techniques. Unsurprisingly, both cc-CDs and N,S-cc-CDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23°, and their interplanar spacing was very similar to the 002 interplanar spacing of graphite, demonstrating their highly-graphitic features (Fig. S6). Notably, Fe-cc-CDs presented similar crystal planes to FeC2O4⋅2H2O and FeS (Fig. S6), due to the dopant of Fe element (Zeng et al., 2020). In addition, XRD pattern (Fig. S6) also indicated that elemental Fe promoted the development of graphitic carbon in Fe-cc-CDs (Zeng et al., 2020). UV–vis absorption spectra indicated that all the resultant CDs had strong absorption capability at ∼270 nm−1, which is a typical absorption feature of CDs (Fig. 2e). FTIR spectra implied that N,S-cc-CDs exhibited a clear C-S absorption peak at 1120 cm−1 and Fe-cc-CDs showcased an apparent Fe-O absorption peak at 579 cm−1 (Fig. 2f). The Fe-cc-CDs also possessed clear C-O, C-N, and C-H peaks (Fig. 2f). These findings suggested that the Fe-cc-CDs owned better water solubility because of the large existence of carboxyls, hydroxyls and mercapto groups. Therefore, it can be concluded that these characterization results comprehensively manifested that the cc-CDs were successfully doped by N, S or Fe elements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Doped cc-CDs used in the biohybrid systems for CO2 reduction
ce:section.ce:para: To disclose the practical CO2-to-formate conversion discrepancies, the doped cc-CDs were then integrated with S. oneidensis MR-1 for bioelectrochemcial CO2 reduction. The successful incorporation of cc-CDs, N,S-cc-CDs and Fe-cc-CDs with S. oneidensis MR-1 is clearly observed by TEM images (Fig. 3 b, c and d), where all the cc-CDs, N,S-cc-CDs and Fe-cc-CDs are taken up by S. oneidensis MR-1 and even being internalized into the interior of the cell, which are unveiled by the presence of 0.26 nm and 0.28 nm lattice fringes in the cell stemming from the (002) plane diffraction of graphitic carbon. In stark comparison, as profiled in Fig. 3a, the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system shows no sign of CDs but only the morphological features of S. oneidensis MR-1, further stressing the excellent biocompatibility between the biomass-derived CDs and S. oneidensis MR-1. As nanomaterials generally show different extents of toxicity to cells, therefore, the cell viability was tested by using the live/dead BacLight bacterial activity assay kit (Xiao et al., 2017). After being co-cultured (doped cc-CDs, 600 mg/L) with S. oneidensis MR-1 for 24 h, the biohybrid system was stained with PI and Syto 9 dyes. It could be clearly observed from Fig. 3e that the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system shows a distinct green fluorescent signal of Syto 9 and a faint red fluorescent signal of PI. In comparison, the bacterial death counts in all the biohybrid system are similar to those in the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system (Fig. 3e), signifying that the addition of CDs contributes negligible effects in affecting the growth of S. oneidensis MR-1, directly confirming the good biocompatibility of as-synthesized CDs in this work. Other studies also point out that CDs generally will not affect the activity of microorganisms as long as within a certain range of concentrations (Liu et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020). Overall, above analyses implied that all the corncob-derived CDs possessed ultralow biotoxicity to S. oneidensis MR-1, therefore, it is expected that both N,S-cc-CDs and Fe-cc-CDs will render a pronounced CO2-to-formate bioelectrosynthesis performance.

Additional toxic experiments regarding the utilization of cc-CDs, N,S-cc-CDs and Fe-cc-CDs for the biohybrid systems were also conducted for insightful understandings, for which the indicators such as proliferation and growth of bacterial colonies were indicated by monitoring the OD600 changes in the cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1, N,S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 and Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems. As expected, the growth of S. oneidensis MR-1 in the CDs-contained biohybrid systems was similar to the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system, also implying no inhibitory effects of CDs on S. oneidensis MR-1 growth (Fig. 4 b). Based on this conclusion, carbon felt was then cultivated in the biohybrid systems to form biofilm as a cathode for MES (Fig. 4a). Specifically, various concentrations of cc-CDs (200 mg/L, 400 mg/L, 800 mg/L and 1000 mg/L) were used in the biohybrid system, among which, the most optimal concertation was 1000 mg/L, as supported by the largest accumulated concentration of formate of 0.50 mmol/L (Fig. 4c). Notably, with 400 mg/L of N,S-cc-CDs used in the N,S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system, 0.71 mmol/L of formate was electrosynthesized (Fig. 4d), which was also higher than the 0.50 mmol/L of formate in the cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 system (in which 1000 mg/mL of cc-CDs was used, Fig. 4c). However, for the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system, the maximum formate production even reached up to 1.22 mmol/L with only 400 mg/L of Fe-cc-CDs used (Fig. 4e), which was 7.7 times higher than that of the cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 system (Fig. 4c). These results underpinned that the doped cc-CDs not only benefited the formate electrosynthesis, but also significantly reduced the amount of CDs usage. Further analysis on the formate production rate (Fig. 4f–h) indicated that the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system exhibited a marked formate production rate of 0.20 mmol/(L⋅h), outperforming previously reported studies (Gupta and Verma, 2022; Kong et al., 2020; Singh et al., 2020; Suo et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2022). Apparently, the integration of doped cc-CDs with electroactive S. oneidensis MR-1 is an effective strategy to improve the MES efficiency for the CO2-to-formate conversion.

As shown in Fig. 4c–e, where a quick accumulation of formate within the first 6 h is clearly observed, followed by a reduced accumulation performance. This accredits to the S. oneidensis MR-1 unable to assimilate formate and compromised activity of S. oneidensis MR-1 after a long-time of MES, therefore, impeding the continuous conversion of CO2 to formate. These observed formate production variations are consistent with the reported study where S. oneidensis MR-1 was utilized to convert CO2 to formate via microbial electrosynthesis (Suo et al., 2019). To demonstrate that the increased formate production was stemming from the internalized-Fe-cc-CDs instead of the increased protein (i.e., biomass), we herein calculated the unit production rate of formate. For which, the highest formate accumulation level was used for the calculation (e.g. cc-CDs of 1000 mg/L, Fe-cc-CDs of 400 mg/L and N, S-cc-CDs of 400 mg/L), with the calculated results listing in Table S2. Unsurprisingly, the formate productivity value points out that the highest unit production rate of formate occurs in the Fe-cc-CDs-internalized cells, indicating that the increase in formate titer is originating from the improved cell activity instead of the enriched protein.

To exclude the possibility of Fe-cc-CDs possessing the marked capability in converting CO2 to formate, additional experiments of exploring Fe-cc-CDs for CO2 electrolysis were then conducted. It can be seen in Fig. S5 that the pure Fe-cc-CDs has no capability in performing CO2 electrolysis, as no formate generated. Despite Fe-cc-CDs owns abundant Fe3+/Fe2+ pairs, which are only able to contribute to the S. oneidensis MR-1 instead of electrode, hence, the promoted CO2-to-fomrate conversion accredits to the constructed Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of CO2 reduction in the biohybrid systems
ce:section.ce:para: Insightful understandings of the roles of CDs in the biohybrid systems are of significant importance, as a result, different electrochemical analysis methods were adopted. According to the cyclic voltammetry in Fig. 5 a, where a pair of symmetrical redox peaks centered at about −0.15 V are observed in all systems and the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system is the most obvious. This redox peak is formed by the direct electron transfer of Cytochrome c (Zhou et al., 2018), and Cytochrome c depends on Fe2+/Fe3+ pairs for completing electron transfer. Importantly, the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system possessed a large fraction of Fe3+ species, which contributed significantly to the strong redox peak (Fig. 5a). The electron transfer capacity was then evaluated using the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Zhang et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 5b, the semicircle diameters (Rct) of the biohybrid systems are all smaller than that of the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system, verifying that the introduction of CDs will promote the electron transfer capability, thus rendering an improved conductivity in the biohybrid systems. Detailed EIS fitting results (as listed in Table S3) indicate that the Rct for the biohybrid system is 3.69 Ω, which is 75.9 % lower than the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system (Rct of 14.7 Ω). The results indicated that the modified cc-CDs would serve as electron transfer shuttle in MES. Consequently, the enhanced bioelectrochemical CO2-to-formate formation in the biohybrid systems is attributed to the improved direct electron transfer efficiency, which is induced by the highly-conductive internalized CDs that boost the electron transfer activity of Cytochrome c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy metabolism of S. Oneidensis MR-1 in the biohybrid systems
ce:section.ce:para: The transmembrane transport of electrons accompanies with energy metabolism, such as the synthesis of ATP and NADH, therefore, the electron transfer and the energy metabolism show inseparable correlations (Carstensen et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018b). As the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system indeed improved the entire electron transfer efficiency, therefore, the roles and mechanisms of Fe-cc-CDs in promoting the energy metabolism need to be insightfully elucidated.

For the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system, the intracellular NADH content reached up to 0.46 μmol/L (Fig. 6 a), which was 5.6-fold higher than the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system (0.07 μmol/L), confirming that the internalization of Fe-cc-CDs greatly boosted the NADH regeneration. As to the NADH/NAD+ ratio, the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1, N,S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 and cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems were respective 0.61, 0.42 and 0.24 (Fig. 6b), which were 4.69, 3.23 and 1.85 times higher than the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system (0.13). These results indicated that the energy metabolism was improved by the addition of CDs, and Fe-cc-CDs contributed considerably than other counterparts. In terms of ATP, the intracellular ATP levels in all the biohybrid systems were higher than the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 one (Fig. 6c), especially the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system that was 2.77 times higher than the S. oneidensis MR-1. On the basis of above analyses, the addition of Fe-cc-CDs significantly enhanced the concentrations of NADH, NAD(H/+) and ATP, therefore, it can be concluded that internalizing CDs into S. oneidensis MR-1 is a plausible and effective way to accelerate the EET efficiency to intensify the energy metabolism, hence, benefiting the CO2-to-formate conversion.

To corroborate above hypothesis, the expression levels of genes related to Mtr pathway and energy metabolism in S. oneidensis MR-1 were then thoroughly analyzed. The reason to detect the genes in Mtr pathway is that Mtr pathway is the main pathway in S. oneidensis MR-1 for electron uptake, leading to a higher EET (Li et al., 2023c). As shown in Fig. 7 a and c, the biohybrid systems overexpress the genes, especially omcA and cctA. Specifically, the omcA is located in the outermembrane for extracellular electron uptake and the cctA located in periplasm enables to transfer electrons from outermembrane to innermembrane. As observed in Fig. 3, TEM image reveals that CDs are homogenously distributed from extracellular to intracellular. In light of above analyses, it can be concluded that the doped cc-CDs will activate the Mtr pathway with an enhanced EET (as supported by the overexpressed omcA and cctA). Additionally, the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system also up-regulated the expression levels of mtrA, mtrC and mtrF (Fig. 7c), which were higher than the N,S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system (Fig. 7a). This is because the MtrA, MtrC and MtrF all belong to the c-type cytochromes, which have the capability to use Fe3+/Fe2+ species for electron transfer, and the Fe-cc-CDs S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system just has massive Fe3+/Fe2+ species (Duhl et al., 2018).

For the energy module, formate dehydrogenase (FDH) is an enzyme-based biocatalyst that is typically used for CO2 bioelectrocatalysis, and its catalytic energy is sourced by NADH and ATP (Chen et al., 2023). Therefore, it can be speculated that the outstanding CO2 reduction performance in the biohybrid systems accredits to the internalization of CDs that positively affects the expression levels of NADH-related genes in S. oneidensis MR-1, giving rise to an improved CO2 reduction efficiency. The synthesis of NADH in S. oneidensis MR-1 is generally considered to consist of three pathways, including the de novo biosynthesis (nadA and nadB), the salvage biosynthesis (nadV and pncC), and the universal biosynthesis (nadD and nadE) (Li et al., 2018b). Among the NADH syntheses pathways, the de novo biosynthesis needs to consume aspartic acid as a substrate, however, no substrates are used in this study, clearly, such related gene expressions can be excluded. As a consequence, detailed gene expressions regarding nadV, pncC, nadD, nadE, nadR and pnnC were then systematically investigated. As profiled in Fig. 7b and d, the biohybrid systems both up-regulated the expression levels of genes especially the nadV, nadR and petC, compared to the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system. As to these overexpressed genes, nadV is a nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase that enables to converse nicotinamide (Nm) to nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN) for NAD+ synthesis, while petC is related to ATP synthesis (Li et al., 2018a; Li et al., 2023c). These overexpressed genes substantiate that the internalization of CDs with S. oneidensis MR-1 is conducive to energy metabolism (as confirmed by the overexpressed genes of nadV, nadR and pnnC), contributing to enhanced CO2 conversion efficiency. On a different note, in the Fe-cc-CDs S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system, the expression levels of pncC and nadD were obviously up-regulated (Fig. 7d), way outperforming the N,S-cc-CDs S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system (Fig. 7b). The function of pncC and nadD is to converse NMN to nicotinic acid adenine dinucleotide (NaAD) (Li et al., 2018a). As the outstanding expression levels of pncC and nadD in the Fe-cc-CDs S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system, therefore, the NADH synthesis pathway is more active than the N,S-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system, in turn leading to a higher formate production performance as confirmed by the experimental CO2 reduction results (Fig. 4d and e).

In conjunction with above analyzed results, the promoted CO2 reduction mechanism in the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system is proposed, as profiled in Fig. 8 . Specifically, the internalization of the highly-conductive and Fe3+-contained Fe-cc-CDs accelerates the electron uptake efficiency through activating the Mtr pathway (see the overexpressed genes of MtrA, MtrB, MtrC, MtrD, MtrE, MtrF, OmcA and CctA in Fig. 7c). Then, the improved EET linking with Fe-cc-CDs promotes the energy metabolism (as supported by the gene overexpression of nadD, nadR, nadV, pncC and petC in Fig. 7d), thus stimulating the intracellular NAD+ synthesis and ATP formation. Subsequently, the massively produced electrons, NADH and ATP are provided to the formate dehydrogenase for CO2 reduction (Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020), hence, ushering a promoted formate electrosynthesis.

Despite the successful cell modification with CDs, the formate titer remains relatively low. Since the MES efficiency is highly associated with biomass and microbial activity, as this work improves the cell activity through the internalization with CDs, therefore, further strategies can focus on electrode modification to improve biomass. For instance, Anwer et al. utilized the metabolic mechanisms of electron transfer to improve the electron transfer efficiency, as practiced by thickening the biofilm on electrodes using reduced graphene oxide and semiconductor SiO2-TiO2, which rendered a 2.1-fold improvement in current density and in turn considerably boosted MES efficiency (Anwer et al., 2023).

In summary, biomass-derived CDs not only exhibited high conductivity, but also showcased excellent biocompatibility with S. oneidensis MR-1. As such, various highly-conductive CDs were internalized into S. oneidensis MR-1 to form different CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid systems for bioelectrochemical CO2 reduction, which led to considerably enhanced CO2-to-formate production. Among the biohybrid systems, the cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system produced the largest formate titer of 0.50 mmol/L, which was more than three times higher than the pure S. oneidensis MR-1 system. Further doping of Fe elements into cc-CDs significantly improved the formate production, with formate in the Fe-cc-CDs-S. oneidensis MR-1 biohybrid system reaching up to 1.22 mmol/L. Such a considerable CO2-to-formate formation enchantment was attributed to the intensified energy metabolism activated by the internalized Fe-cc-CDs, which was substantiated by the largely-synthesized intracellular NAD+, ATP and overexpression level of energy-related genes. Thus, large electrons, NADH and ATP can be provided to the formate dehydrogenase for efficient CO2-to-formate conversion. This work underscores that intensifying the energy metabolism in microorganisms by introducing conductive nanomaterials is an effective strategy to boost the bioelectrochemical CO2 conversion efficiency.
